questions.question: Explain why standard TCP Reno congestion control is insufficient for high-speed and long-distance networks, leading to the development of variants like TCP Hybla.
keyPoints: TCP Reno uses AIMD (Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease) which is inefficient on high bandwidth-delay product networks
Slow convergence time to reach available bandwidth in long-distance connections
Multiple packet losses occur due to large congestion windows before detection
TCP Hybla aims to achieve fairness and efficiency by normalizing congestion window growth based on RTT

questions.question: Describe the key differences between loss-based congestion control (TCP CUBIC) and model-based congestion control (TCP BBR) in terms of how they detect and respond to network congestion.
keyPoints: Loss-based methods (CUBIC) infer congestion from packet losses, operating after queues fill up
BBR builds explicit model of bottleneck bandwidth and RTT to operate at equilibrium point
BBR aims to minimize queuing delay rather than filling buffers
BBR achieves higher throughput with lower latency compared to loss-based variants

questions.question: Compare and contrast TCP BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and Round-trip propagation time) with TCP CUBIC and TCP Compound TCP. Discuss their respective approaches to congestion window management, fairness, and suitability for different network scenarios.
keyPoints: BBR uses rate-based pacing and models bandwidth-RTT product, while CUBIC uses cubic function and Compound TCP uses hybrid approach of delay-based and loss-based
BBR operates at point of minimum RTT avoiding bufferbloat, CUBIC fills buffers leading to higher queuing delays
Fairness issues: BBR may be unfair to loss-based flows in shared bottlenecks; CUBIC is designed for high-speed networks; Compound TCP balances between Reno and Vegas
BBR excels in high bandwidth-delay product and buffer-filled networks; CUBIC suitable for long-haul high-speed links; Compound TCP optimal for mixed wired/wireless environments

questions.question: Explain the working mechanism of TCP BBR's congestion control algorithm. Detail how BBR measures bottleneck bandwidth and round-trip propagation time, and describe the states in its state machine along with how it handles packet loss and bandwidth changes.
keyPoints: BBR uses packet delivery rate measurement: divides packets delivered by time interval to estimate bottleneck bandwidth
MinRTT tracking records minimum RTT over time window to estimate propagation delay

State machine: STARTUP (exponential growth to find bandwidth), DRAIN (drain excess queue), PROBE_BW (cycle through pacing gains to test for more bandwidth), PROBE_RTT (temporarily reduces cwnd to measure MinRTT)
BBR responds to loss differently than loss-based methods: uses pacing to maintain steady throughput, and detects bandwidth changes through delivery rate variations rather than loss events
